title: Lynda Resnick on 21st Century Leadership | Big Think
text:
Each division has its own president.
They're standalone companies. So Fiji
has a president and Palm has a and so
forth and so on.
And although we use the parent company
for legal and for um uh we have an
in-house advertising agency called the
fire station and uh they serve all the
groups and the PR is central but then
they have people assigned and so forth
but each company runs in a very
entrepreneurial way and one of the
things that's rewarded at our companies
is the entrepreneurial spirit. So they
know that that's how you do well.
At this point, what I do is I have
meetings with each group once a week or
more if necessary. I mean, I talk to
them every day, but I go in and review
the work and so forth. With some
companies, you know, companies are like
people.
They have a life cycle and so um Palm is
just getting out of their toddler stage
right now but Tela is mature and I've
just had to reinvent that company. So I
am more involved with Telora let's say I
used to be the president of Telllora
like 24 years ago 25 years ago but uh I
have a great president a great staff
there but right now they need me a
little more the nut business um doesn't
do as much advertising and marketing as
the other businesses and so and I have a
very strong guy there and
he's much more salesoriented and and um
does a lot with the international
expansion. So he's fine. He doesn't need
me so much. And so I go where I'm needed
and I tend to have very big meetings
these days, you know, at this point in
my life um with a lot of people on board
so they all get the message and we go
through the agenda. And I'm very uh
detail oriented. God is in the details.
I'm It's awful. I can't help it.
Everything has the same importance.
I try to. That's why sometimes my engine
uh runs down. People say to my husband,
"Oh, Linda has so much energy." He says,
"You should see her at 9:30 at night."
Well, it's very important that we can
work together, obviously. Um, but I have
learned the hard way that you can spend
six months interviewing somebody and
you'll know more the first three hours
they're at work than you will ever know
from those interviews. And so you try to
do your due diligence. You know, it's
very hard for people to tell you the
truth where they've worked before, but
there are ways to find out um how
successful they were because it's a
small world. And you know, and thank God
for Google. And um you just go with your
instinct, I'm afraid, in the end, you
just do.
